text: Hello friends. This is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy.
A large myoma, approximately twelve centimeters in size, is visible. The surgical team is using a supraumbilical port for access.

The surgeon introduces the irrigation suction device and performs the hanging drop test, which involves observing the drop of fluid at the tip of the instrument to confirm its position within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port to insert the laparoscopic telescope for visualizing the large myoma.

The surgeon positions the supraumbilical port at least ten centimeters above the umbilicus to prevent the uterine manipulator or myoma screw from touching the telescope. The preset pressure is set to fifteen with a flow rate of one liter per minute. The trocar is inserted, followed by the introduction of the telescope.

The surgeon identifies the plane of dissection around a large intramural myoma located at the apex of the uterus. The uterus exhibits a marble white appearance due to the effect of vasopressin. A myoma screw is applied, and the serous layer is cut in a crescent shape using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to hold the large intramural myoma, facilitating its separation from the uterine tissue. The myoma screw is operated with the left hand.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel with the right hand to cut the serous layer in a crescent shape. Depending on the port placement, the incision is made horizontally for ipsilateral ports or vertically for contralateral ports.

The surgeon utilizes an ipsilateral port for the procedure, while an assistant uses a contralateral port to hold the myoma screw in place, facilitating the manipulation of the myoma during the myomectomy.
The surgeon makes the initial incision on the uterus to access the large intramural myoma, which is visibly protruding from the uterine muscle, facilitating its separation.

The surgeon performs a concave incision around the myoma using a harmonic device, creating a crescent-shaped cut from the opposite side of the myoma screw. The large intramural myoma visibly protrudes from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the muscle and serosa on the opposite side of the myoma, ensuring that the myoma itself is not cut.

The surgeon uses the myoma screw with the left hand and suction irrigation with the right hand to enucleate the myoma. The suction irrigation provides necessary force to avoid breaking the instrument while carefully separating the myoma from the surrounding uterine tissue.

The surgeon has reached the base of the myoma and is gently separating the feeding vessels to enucleate the myoma. Approximately ninety percent of the myoma is visible outside the uterine wall, and the remaining layer is being separated using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies and coagulates any bleeding vessels using bipolar or ligature after separating the uterus. The entire myoma is now enucleated, and the surgeon begins suturing the uterine muscle, starting with the first inner layer in a triple-layer closure technique.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal continuous suturing to approximate the muscle layers of the uterus, effectively stopping any bleeding from the surgical site.
The surgeon tightens the initial suture on the left side, taking two to three bites to stop the bleeding. The assistant holds the suture, providing traction to secure the tissue.
The assistant holds the suture with an instrument through the contralateral port, providing traction as the surgeon continues with the suturing process.

The surgeon observes minor bleeding collecting over the bowel and omentum, which appears larger due to laparoscopic magnification. Continuous suturing of the inner muscle layers is resumed to control the bleeding.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing, starting on the right side and progressing to the left, pulling the suture to approximate the muscle layers.
The surgeon tightens the intracorporeal continuous suture, effectively approximating the inner muscle layer and visibly reducing the bleeding.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the inner muscle layer, preparing to perform suction irrigation to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the inner muscle layer and begins suturing the outer layer. The needle is passed through the serosa, and the Maryland forceps hold the suture as the surgeon reverses direction, tightening the outer layer from left to right with continuous suturing.
The surgeon tightens the second layer of sutures, visibly approximating the muscle tissue layers during the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the continuous suturing of the outer muscle layer, reaching the right end and tightening the suture. The suture tail is used to terminate the closure, ensuring the muscle layer is securely approximated.

The surgeon terminates the second layer of suturing using the same tail, followed by continuous suturing with a Dandy jamming knot to reinforce the layer. The surgical field shows no bleeding, and the muscle is being closed to prevent future complications such as uterine rupture during pregnancy.

The surgeon performs the final layer of suturing using a Dundee jamming knot with continuous interlocking sutures. The suture is passed through the loop, pulled out, and locked, while the left hand holds the suture for optimal traction. The right hand continues the suturing to the opposite side, interlocking with each pass.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the outer layer, using the same suture to approximate the tissue as the needle is passed through the serosa.
The surgeon performs interlocking continuous suturing on the uterine tissue, taking bites between previous sutures without using a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon slowly reaches the opposite side of the uterus, using the tail of the previous layer's suture to terminate the outer layer suture. The interlocking suture technique is applied, with the left hand holding the suture and the right hand using a needle holder to tighten it. The uterus appears well-shaped, with a slight bulge visible.
It will be alright. This patient has only one myoma, large myoma, so that is good thing. Sometime if the surrounding sub-centimeter is small myoma, then sometime it is difficult to take it.
The surgeon terminates the outer suture layer using a surgical knot, completing the interlocking suturing technique on the uterus.

The surgeon carefully removes the needle after completing the final knot of the suture. Suction irrigation is performed to clear the surgical field, revealing minimal bleeding from the uterus, which was initially due to surface bleeding.

The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the needle through a five-millimeter port. The sutures are held near the needle and hidden in the cannula for removal. Final suction irrigation is performed to clean residual blood from the surgical field. Morselation is applied to manage the large myoma.

The surgeon begins the process of morselation, using a specialized instrument to fragment the large myoma within the uterine cavity. The procedure involves careful manipulation to reduce the size of the myoma for easier removal.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a morselator, a device used to fragment and remove large myomas. The procedure is performed with a MorSafe endo bag to ensure safety, especially when there is suspicion of malignancy.
